annotations.label: Symmetry of a geometric object

anchor.snippet: an object has symmetry if there is an operation or transformation

annotations.note: The general notion of transformations leaving an object invariant is captured abstractly by the stabilizer of a point under a group action, but no dedicated geometric-symmetry definition exists.

annotations.label: Rotational symmetry (circle)

anchor.snippet: A circle is thus said to be symmetric under rotation or to have rotational symmetry

annotations.note: Rotations are formalized (e.g. `Orientation.rotation`), but the specific statement that a circle is invariant under all rotations is not.

annotations.label: Reflectional symmetry / line symmetry

anchor.snippet: the figure is said to have reflectional symmetry or line symmetry

annotations.note: Reflection across a subspace is formalized, but reflectional symmetry as a property of a figure is not defined.

annotations.label: Symmetry group of an object

anchor.snippet: the set of transforms under which an object is symmetric form a mathematical group

annotations.note: `MulAction.stabilizer` is exactly the (sub)group of transformations fixing an object, generalizing the symmetry group.

annotations.label: Euclidean group of isometries

anchor.snippet: distance-preserving transformations in space commonly referred to

annotations.note: Distance-preserving bijections (`IsometryEquiv`) form a group under composition, but the Euclidean group is not packaged under that name.

annotations.label: Symmetric under an isometric transformation

anchor.snippet: a geometric object is said to be symmetric if, after transformation, the object is indistinguishable

annotations.note: Isometric transformations are formalized via `Isometry`/`IsometryEquiv`, but invariance of an object under one is not given as a definition.

annotations.label: Cartan–Dieudonné theorem

anchor.snippet: an orthogonal transformation in n -dimensional space can be represented by the composition of at most

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the real inner-product-space case (each orthogonal map is a product of at most `finrank` reflections), explicitly noted as a special case of Cartan–Dieudonné.

annotations.label: Reflectional symmetry

anchor.snippet: is symmetry with respect to reflection

annotations.note: Reflection is formalized as an isometry, but reflectional symmetry of a figure is not a defined property.

annotations.label: Axis of symmetry

anchor.snippet: The axis of symmetry of a two-dimensional figure is a line such that

annotations.note: Reflection across an affine subspace exists, but the notion of an axis/line of symmetry of a figure is not defined.

annotations.label: Axes of symmetry of square and circle

anchor.snippet: a square has four axes of symmetry

annotations.note: No statement about counting axes of symmetry of squares or circles is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vertical symmetry of letter T

anchor.snippet: If the letter T is reflected along a vertical axis, it appears the same

annotations.note: This illustrative example about a letter glyph has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Triangles and quadrilaterals with reflection symmetry

anchor.snippet: The triangles with reflection symmetry are isosceles

annotations.note: No classification of triangles/quadrilaterals by reflection symmetry is formalized.

annotations.label: Reflection symmetry group isomorphic to C_s

anchor.snippet: the symmetry group is isomorphic with C s

annotations.note: Reflection is proven involutive (hence generates an order-2 group), but the isomorphism to C_s is not stated.

annotations.label: Involutive isometries generalizing reflection

anchor.section: Point reflection and other involutive isometries
anchor.snippet: Reflection symmetry can be generalized to other isometries of m -dimensional space which are involutions

annotations.note: Isometric involutions exist (`Function.Involutive` applied to reflections), but a general class of involutive isometries is not defined as such.

annotations.label: Point reflection / inversion through a point

anchor.section: Point reflection and other involutive isometries
anchor.snippet: such a transformation is known as a point reflection , or an inversion through a point

annotations.note: Point reflection through a point is defined as an affine isometric automorphism `AffineIsometryEquiv.pointReflection`.

annotations.label: Orientation preserved iff k even

anchor.section: Point reflection and other involutive isometries
anchor.snippet: preserves orientation if and only if k is an even number

annotations.note: `det_reflection` gives the determinant of a reflection as `(-1)^finrank`, the key fact behind orientation parity, but the iff-k-even statement is not packaged.

annotations.label: Rotational symmetry

anchor.snippet: Rotational symmetry is symmetry with respect to some or all rotations

annotations.note: Rotations are formalized, but rotational symmetry as an invariance property is not defined.

annotations.label: All-point rotations imply translational symmetry

anchor.snippet: implies translational symmetry with respect to all translations

annotations.note: The implication from rotational symmetry about every point to translational symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Rotations about a point form SO(m)

anchor.snippet: These rotations form the special orthogonal group SO( m )

annotations.note: The special orthogonal group SO(m) is defined, but the identification of geometric rotations about a point with SO(m) is not proven.

annotations.label: Noether: rotational symmetry and angular momentum

anchor.snippet: rotational symmetry of a physical system is equivalent to the angular momentum conservation law

annotations.note: Noether's theorem linking rotational symmetry to angular-momentum conservation (physics) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Translational symmetry

anchor.snippet: Translational symmetry leaves an object invariant under a discrete or continuous group of translations

annotations.note: Translations are formalized (`constVAdd`) and form a group, but translational symmetry as an invariance property is not defined.

annotations.label: Glide reflection symmetry

anchor.snippet: means that a reflection in a line or plane combined with a translation

annotations.note: Glide reflection is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Composition of two glide reflections

anchor.snippet: The composition of two glide reflections results in a translation symmetry with twice the translation vector

annotations.note: No statement about composing glide reflections exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rotoreflection / improper rotation

anchor.snippet: is a rotation about an axis combined with reflection in a plane perpendicular to that axis

annotations.note: Rotoreflection (improper rotation) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Screw axis (rotary translation)

anchor.snippet: a screw axis (or rotary translation) is a combination of a rotation and a translation along the rotation axis

annotations.note: The screw axis / rotary translation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Helical symmetry

anchor.snippet: Helical symmetry is the kind of symmetry seen in everyday objects

annotations.note: Helical symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Infinite helical symmetry

anchor.snippet: If there are no distinguishing features along the length of a helix

annotations.note: Infinite helical symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: n-fold helical symmetry

anchor.snippet: If the requirement that every cross section of the helical object be identical is relaxed

annotations.note: n-fold helical symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-repeating helical symmetry

anchor.snippet: This is the case in which the angle of rotation

annotations.note: Non-repeating helical symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Double rotation symmetry

anchor.snippet: a double rotation symmetry can be generated as the composite of two orthogonal rotations

annotations.note: Double rotation symmetry (4D) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-isometric geometric symmetry

anchor.snippet: A wider definition of geometric symmetry allows operations from a larger group than the Euclidean group of isometries

annotations.note: Invariance under arbitrary group actions (beyond isometries) is captured by `MulAction.stabilizer`, but no geometric symmetry framework is built on it.

annotations.label: Klein's Erlangen program

anchor.snippet: each possible group of symmetries defines a geometry in which objects that are related by a member of the symmetry group are considered to be equivalent

annotations.note: Klein's Erlangen program is not formalized as a framework in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scale symmetry

anchor.snippet: Scale symmetry means that if an object is expanded or reduced in size, the new object has the same properties as the original

annotations.note: Scaling maps (`homothety`) are formalized, but scale invariance as a symmetry property is not.

annotations.label: Fractals

anchor.snippet: fractals are a mathematical concept in which the structure of a complex form looks similar at any degree of magnification

annotations.note: Fractals / self-similarity are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geometry and its group of symmetries (Klein)

anchor.snippet: With every geometry, Felix Klein associated an underlying group of symmetries

annotations.note: Klein's association of a geometry with a symmetry group is not formalized.

annotations.label: Model geometry (Thurston)

anchor.snippet: A model geometry is a simply connected smooth manifold X together with a transitive action of a Lie group G

annotations.note: Thurston model geometries are not formalized, though component notions (Lie groups, transitive actions, manifolds) exist separately.

annotations.label: Maximal model geometry

anchor.snippet: A model geometry is called maximal if G is maximal among groups acting smoothly and transitively

annotations.note: Maximal model geometry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Geometric structure on a manifold

anchor.snippet: A geometric structure on a manifold M is a diffeomorphism from M to

annotations.note: Geometric structures in Thurston's sense are not formalized.

annotations.label: Model geometry relevant to geometrization conjecture

anchor.snippet: is relevant to the geometrization conjecture if it is maximal

annotations.note: Relevance to the geometrization conjecture is not formalized.

annotations.label: Thurston's classification of 8 model geometries

anchor.snippet: Thurston classified the 8 model geometries satisfying these conditions

annotations.note: Thurston's classification of the eight 3-dimensional model geometries is not in Mathlib.
